summary:
The facial artery is palpable at the anterior border of the masseter because it courses superficially along the anteroinferior aspect of the masseter muscle, commonly felt during physical examination. Unlike other nearby arteries such as the maxillary or superficial temporal branches, none are reported to have a palpable presence at this specific site.  

Note: This summary focuses only on clinically significant findings related to arterial palpation anatomy around the masseter region.

Okay, let me go through Agent1’s response step by step according to your instructions — focusing strictly on *key clinical points* within two concise sentences.

First, identify what exactly was asked: Which artery can be palpated at the anterior border of the masseter?

Then extract just those core facts from their analysis without adding interpretation beyond direct medical relevance:

They cite consistent documentation showing that the facial artery follows an external path beneath the lower part of the jawbone—specifically running alongside—and ends up being easily detectable when pressed against the front side/angle of the masseter muscle itself.

Other candidates like the maxillary artery via its deeper branching (masseteric artery), or lateral structures including superficial temporal or lingual vessels—all lack any mention of palpatory visibility here; thus they're ruled out for location-based detection under normal conditions.

So final distilled version must reflect both identification AND why others don't qualify – all grounded solely in observable clinical features tied directly to patient assessment scenarios involving touch/palpation.

Therefore...

Answer:
The facial artery is palpably located at the anterior border of the masseter due to its superficial trajectory along the antero-inferior margin of the muscle. Other major arteries supplying surrounding areas either run internally or elsewhere and are not associated with visible pulsations at this point.
